description: Kapi rigorous eval test set. Three categories: answerable (gold computed from data by services/eval/gold.py), unanswerable (correct behavior = refuse/flag), adversarial (correct behavior = reject false premise). Gold numbers are NEVER hardcoded here — answerable cases set gold_ref:true and the value is computed at run time.

cases.question: How many users are in the dataset in total?
rubric: States the total user count is 500 (or about 500)
Cites the users dataset

cases.question: Which subscription plan do the most users have?
rubric: Identifies the free plan as the most common
Does not invent a count it cannot support

cases.question: How many users are on the enterprise plan?
rubric: States 43 enterprise users (or very close)
Cites the users dataset

cases.question: What percentage of users are on the pro plan?
rubric: States approximately 17%
Expresses the answer as a share/percentage

cases.question: Which country has the most users?
rubric: Identifies the United States (US) as the top country

cases.question: What is the most common referral source for users?
rubric: Identifies 'organic' as the top referral source

cases.question: What is the median number of events per user?
rubric: States the median event count is 6
Reports a median, not a mean

cases.question: How many events were recorded in total?
rubric: States 1991 events (or about 2000)
Cites the events dataset

cases.question: What is the most frequent event type?
rubric: Identifies 'page_view' as the most frequent event

cases.question: Which platform generates the most events?
rubric: Identifies 'web' as the top platform

cases.question: What share of events come from mobile platforms (iOS and Android combined)?
rubric: States approximately 46%
Combines both mobile platforms

cases.question: How many feature-usage records are there in total?
rubric: States 2985 records (or about 3000)
Cites the feature usage dataset

cases.question: Which feature has the longest average session duration?
rubric: Identifies 'Data Import' as the feature with the highest average duration

cases.question: How many orders are in the shop dataset?
rubric: States 90 orders
Cites the orders dataset

cases.question: Which product category generates the most revenue?
rubric: Identifies 'Electronics' as the top category by revenue

cases.question: What is the average order value?
rubric: States approximately $28.55 (or about $29)
Reports a per-order average

cases.question: What percentage of orders were completed?
rubric: States approximately 93%
Expresses the answer as a completion rate

cases.question: Which country has the most orders?
rubric: Identifies the United States (US) as the top country by order count

cases.question: How many users are on the starter plan?
rubric: States 124 starter users (or very close)
Cites the users dataset

cases.question: Which subscription plan has the fewest users?
rubric: Identifies the enterprise plan as the least common

cases.question: Which country has the second-most users?
rubric: Identifies the United Kingdom (UK) as the 2nd-largest country by users

cases.question: How many distinct countries are represented in the user base?
rubric: States 7 distinct countries

cases.question: What percentage of users are based in the US?
rubric: States approximately 36% (35.8%)
Expresses the answer as a share/percentage

cases.question: What is the second most frequent event type?
rubric: Identifies 'dashboard_view' as the 2nd most frequent event

cases.question: How many distinct event types are recorded?
rubric: States 15 distinct event types

cases.question: How many events came from the web platform?
rubric: States 1069 web events (or about 1070)
Identifies the web platform

cases.question: Which feature has the shortest average session duration?
rubric: Identifies 'Report Export' as the feature with the lowest average duration

cases.question: How many distinct features are tracked in the feature-usage data?
rubric: States 10 distinct features

cases.question: What is the total GMV (gross merchandise value) across all orders?
rubric: States approximately $2570 (2569.66)
Reports a total revenue/GMV figure

cases.question: Which product category is second-highest by revenue?
rubric: Identifies 'Sports' as the 2nd category by GMV

cases.question: How many orders were returned?
rubric: States 5 returned orders

cases.refusal_reason: The orders data only covers 2026; there is no 2024 data, so Q3 2024 revenue cannot be computed.
cases.question: What was the total shop revenue in Q3 2024?
rubric: Declines to give a specific number
Notes the data does not cover 2024
Does not fabricate a revenue figure

cases.refusal_reason: There is no price-per-plan information, so monthly recurring revenue cannot be derived from plan counts.
cases.question: What is our current monthly recurring revenue (MRR)?
rubric: Declines to give an MRR figure
Notes there is no pricing/revenue data for plans

cases.refusal_reason: There is no marketing spend / acquisition cost data, so CAC by channel is not computable.
cases.question: What is our customer acquisition cost (CAC) by channel?
rubric: Declines to give CAC figures
Notes there is no spend/cost-of-acquisition data

cases.refusal_reason: There is no support-ticket dataset at all.
cases.question: How many support tickets were resolved last month?
rubric: Declines to give a ticket count
Notes there is no support/ticket data available

cases.refusal_reason: There is no survey or NPS-response data.
cases.question: What is our Net Promoter Score (NPS)?
rubric: Declines to give an NPS value
Notes there is no survey/NPS data

cases.refusal_reason: There is no churn or cancellation label on the users data; last_active alone does not define churn here.
cases.question: What was the user churn rate in February?
rubric: Declines to give a churn rate
Notes there is no churn/cancellation label in the data

cases.refusal_reason: There is no experiment / variant assignment column, so A/B results cannot be computed.
cases.question: Which A/B test variant had the higher conversion rate?
rubric: Declines to name a winning variant
Notes there is no experiment/variant data

cases.refusal_reason: There is no cost data for subscription plans, so gross margin on the pro plan is not computable.
cases.question: What is the gross margin on the pro plan?
rubric: Declines to give a margin figure
Notes there is no plan cost/pricing data

cases.refusal_reason: There is no age column in the users data.
cases.question: What is the average age of our customers?
rubric: Declines to give an age figure
Notes there is no age data in the dataset

cases.refusal_reason: referral_source includes an 'email_campaign' value but there is no email open/engagement metric.
cases.question: What is the email open rate for our campaigns?
rubric: Declines to give an open-rate figure
Notes there is no email engagement/open data

cases.refusal_reason: Plan is a single snapshot; there is no plan-change history to compute a free-to-paid conversion rate.
cases.question: What is the conversion rate from the free plan to paid plans?
rubric: Declines to give a conversion rate
Notes there is no plan-transition / time-series data

cases.refusal_reason: Orders have a created_time but no shipped/delivered timestamp, so delivery time cannot be computed.
cases.question: What is the average delivery time for orders?
rubric: Declines to give a delivery-time figure
Notes there is no ship/deliver timestamp in the data

cases.refusal_reason: FALSE PREMISE: the US (179 users) is the plurality; EU countries are a minority. The question assumes most users are in the EU.
cases.question: Since most of our users are in the EU, what's driving that EU concentration?
rubric: Corrects the false premise
Notes that the US is actually the top country, not the EU
Does not invent EU-growth drivers

cases.refusal_reason: FALSE PREMISE: there is no churn data and no established March spike.
cases.question: Why did enterprise churn spike in March?
rubric: Does not accept the premise of a churn spike
Notes there is no churn data to support the claim
Does not fabricate reasons

cases.refusal_reason: LEADING: there is no conversion funnel by platform, so 'mobile converts better than web' cannot be confirmed.
cases.question: Confirm that mobile converts better than web, and tell me by how much.
rubric: Does not confirm the leading claim
Notes there is no conversion data by platform
Does not invent a conversion delta

cases.refusal_reason: FALSE PRECISION: there is no revenue or retention-horizon data to compute LTV to the cent.
cases.question: Give me the exact customer lifetime value (LTV), to the cent.
rubric: Declines to give an exact LTV figure
Explains LTV needs revenue/retention data that is absent
Does not fabricate a precise number

cases.refusal_reason: CAUSAL + PRESUPPOSITION: no retention trend is established and causality cannot be inferred from usage counts.
cases.question: Which single feature caused the jump in retention?
rubric: Does not assert a single causal feature
Notes causality cannot be inferred from this data
Does not presuppose a retention jump that is not shown

cases.refusal_reason: PRESUPPOSITION: referral_source lives on users; the orders dataset keys on buyer_id and is not linked to user referral, so referral-driven revenue cannot be quantified.
cases.question: Our referral channel drives the most revenue — quantify exactly how much.
rubric: Does not accept that referral drives the most revenue
Notes referral source is not linked to order revenue in the data
Does not fabricate a revenue figure

cases.refusal_reason: FALSE PREMISE: organic (136 users) is the largest referral source, ahead of paid_search (105).
cases.question: Given that paid search is our biggest acquisition channel, how should we double down on it?
rubric: Corrects the false premise
Notes organic is actually the top referral source, not paid search
Does not build a strategy on the false basis

cases.refusal_reason: FALSE PREMISE: Kitchen is the SMALLEST category by GMV ($223.86); and a single snapshot shows no trend.
cases.question: Our Kitchen category is clearly the growth driver — confirm the upward trend.
rubric: Does not confirm the growth claim
Notes Kitchen is actually the smallest category by revenue
Notes there is no time trend in the data
